screening_test: RBC Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity test; Atropine challenge (clinical — no tachycardia = OP confirmed)
confirmatory_test: GC-MS of urine for alkyl phosphate metabolites (TCPy for chlorpyrifos; DMAP/DEAP for others)
supporting_tests: Plasma pseudocholinesterase (depressed >20%); Serial RBC AChE monitoring; ECG (QTc prolongation)
toxic_threshold: RBC AChE depressed >20% = significant; >50% = severe; Urine metabolites by GC-MS (qualitative + quantitative)
critical_note: Atropine challenge is a CLINICAL confirmatory step — include as output option in DSS when OP is top prediction; GC-MS distinguishes specific OP compound (important for legal cases)
priority_sample: Blood (RBC AChE); Urine (GC-MS metabolites); Gastric lavage

screening_test: Silver nitrate paper test — paper turns BLACK over gastric contents (phosphine gas + AgNO3); Gutzeit Test
confirmatory_test: GC headspace analysis of gastric contents / blood for phosphine (PH3); Blood phosphine level
supporting_tests: Serum Magnesium (low — specific to AlP); ABG (severe metabolic acidosis); ECG (arrhythmia pattern); Serum lactate
toxic_threshold: Blood phosphine detectable; Metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1 = severe); Mg2+ <0.7 mmol/L (low) specific to AlP toxicity
critical_note: Silver nitrate paper result (BLACK) should be flagged as HIGH CONFIDENCE in DSS output when AlP is predicted — this is a near-pathognomonic bedside test in Indian FSL practice
priority_sample: Gastric lavage (primary — highest phosphine); Blood (phosphine); Lung tissue (post-mortem)

screening_test: Reinsch Test — silver-grey to black metallic deposit on copper strip; Marsh's Test — black metallic mirror; LOD: As 1 µg/mL (urine)
confirmatory_test: Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS) or ICP-MS on hair / urine / blood / viscera; Speciation (inorganic vs organic arsenic by HPLC-ICP-MS)
supporting_tests: Urine arsenic speciation; Hair segmental analysis (maps timeline); Renal function (acute); Liver enzymes
toxic_threshold: Hair arsenic >1 µg/g = abnormal; Urine >50 µg/L = toxic; Blood >100 µg/L = toxic; Hair 1 cm = 1 month exposure timeline
critical_note: HAIR IS GOLD STANDARD for chronic/homicidal cases — segmental analysis gives month-by-month exposure history; DSS should flag hair as priority sample when chronic timeline is entered
priority_sample: Hair (1 cm = 1 month; GOLD for chronic); Nails; Urine (acute); Blood; Viscera (liver, kidney)

screening_test: Reinsch Test — black/grey mirror deposit on copper strip; Differentiate from As: As deposit dissolves in dilute HCl; Hg does NOT
confirmatory_test: AAS or ICP-MS on blood / urine / hair; Speciation (inorganic vs organic methyl mercury)
supporting_tests: Urine mercury (24-hr collection preferred); Hair (organic/methyl Hg); Renal function (proteinuria); Nerve conduction study (chronic neuropathy)
toxic_threshold: Blood Hg >100 µg/L = toxic; Urine Hg >50 µg/L = toxic; Hair >1 µg/g = elevated (methyl mercury exposure)
critical_note: Reinsch HCl dissolution test is KEY to differentiate Hg from As — both give black deposit; DSS should output this differentiating step when both metals are in top predictions
priority_sample: Blood; Urine (24-hr); Hair (organic Hg); Gastric contents

screening_test: Urine immunoassay (ELISA) — detects opiates rapidly; Marquis reagent: purple-red → violet → blue
confirmatory_test: GC-MS or LC-MS/MS on blood / urine; 6-MAM (6-monoacetyl- morphine) by LC-MS/MS = specific for HEROIN (not morphine alone)
supporting_tests: Blood morphine level (quantitative); Naloxone challenge (clinical — reversal confirms opioid); Hair analysis (chronic use / drug trafficking)
toxic_threshold: Blood morphine >0.5 mg/L = toxic; >1 mg/L = potentially fatal; 6-MAM in urine/blood = HEROIN specific (not present in morphine overdose)
critical_note: 6-MAM is the KEY differentiator between heroin and morphine in the DSS output — flag this test specifically when IV route is entered with opioid prediction; Naloxone reversal is clinical confirmation
priority_sample: Blood (morphine level); Urine (immunoassay + GC-MS); Hair (chronic use); Gastric lavage (oral)

screening_test: Mandelin's Test — deep violet-blue turning to YELLOW (specific colour sequence for strychnine)
confirmatory_test: HPLC-UV or GC-MS on gastric contents / urine / blood; LC-MS/MS (most sensitive)
supporting_tests: Blood strychnine level (quantitative); Urine confirmation; EMG (muscle activity); Rhabdomyolysis panel (CK, creatinine)
toxic_threshold: Blood strychnine >1 mg/L = toxic; >2 mg/L = severe; Gastric contents highest concentration early; Rabdomyolysis: CK >1000 IU/L
critical_note: GASTRIC CONTENTS are the most important sample early (highest concentration before absorption) — DSS should prioritise gastric lavage as sample recommendation when strychnine is predicted and timeline is early/acute
priority_sample: Gastric contents (PRIMARY — early); Blood; Urine; Viscera (liver)

screening_test: Copper sulphate–pyridine test — lilac-blue precipitate; Prussian Blue test — deep blue precipitate; Fujiwara Test — blue
confirmatory_test: Conway microdiffusion method; Ion-selective electrode (cyanide-specific); GC headspace of blood for HCN
supporting_tests: Blood lactate (severe lactic acidosis); Arterial blood gas (metabolic acidosis); Venous pO2 (high — paradoxical venous arterialization)
toxic_threshold: Blood cyanide >1 mg/L = toxic; >3 mg/L = potentially lethal; Venous pO2 paradoxically HIGH (cells cannot utilise O2); Lactate >10 mmol/L
critical_note: WHOLE BLOOD ONLY — cyanide unstable in serum; DSS must output this critical sample note; Venous pO2 being HIGH (not low) is a key biochemical differentiator from other causes of collapse — include in DSS output note
priority_sample: WHOLE blood (not serum — cyanide unstable); Gastric contents; Post-mortem lung tissue; Brain (post-mortem)

screening_test: Visual — blood/lividity appears CHERRY-RED; CO-oximetry (COHb% directly — spectrophotometric); NOT a colour reagent test
confirmatory_test: CO-oximetry (co-oximeter machine — gold standard); Spectrophotometry (3-wavelength method); Post-mortem: COHb% preserved in blood
supporting_tests: Standard ABG pO2 is FALSELY NORMAL — do NOT use; Pulse oximetry also FALSELY NORMAL; Only co-oximeter gives COHb% correctly
toxic_threshold: COHb <3% = normal (non-smoker); COHb <10% = normal (smoker); COHb >20% = toxic; COHb >40% = severe; COHb >50% = potentially lethal
critical_note: CRITICAL DSS NOTE: Standard ABG pO2 and pulse oximetry are NORMAL and MISLEADING in CO poisoning — DSS output should explicitly state: 'Only co-oximetry reveals COHb — standard pO2 will be falsely normal'; This is a common diagnostic error
priority_sample: Blood (ante-mortem — co-oximetry URGENT); Post-mortem femoral vein blood (COHb preserved PM); Vitreous humour

screening_test: Ethanol: Chromic acid test — orange to green; Breathalyser (ante-mortem); Enzymatic assay (alcohol dehydrogenase method)
confirmatory_test: GC-FID or GC headspace (GC-HS) — gold standard for both ethanol and methanol; Formate level (serum) — methanol specific; EtG (Ethyl glucuronide) in urine — ethanol up to 80 hrs
supporting_tests: ABG (metabolic acidosis — methanol); Serum osmolal gap (elevated in methanol); Optic nerve fundoscopy (methanol — papilloedema); Anion gap (high = methanol acidosis)
toxic_threshold: Ethanol BAC >400 mg/dL = lethal; Methanol >20 mg/dL = toxic; Formate >20 mg/dL = severe methanol toxicity; Osmal gap >10 mOsm/kg = suspicious
critical_note: Methanol and ethanol often co-ingested in hooch — DSS should recommend BOTH levels when Ethanol/Methanol is predicted; Vitreous humour preferred for post-mortem (resists PMR — post-mortem redistribution); Formate level is key methanol-specific test
priority_sample: Blood (BAC; methanol; formate — urgent); Vitreous humour (post-mortem — GOLD); Urine (EtG up to 80 hrs); Gastric contents

screening_test: FPN Test (Ferric chloride– Perchloric–Nitric acid) — pink to red to violet; Urine naked eye — CHOCOLATE-BROWN (myoglobinuria — visual diagnostic)
confirmatory_test: HPLC or GC-MS on blood / urine for PPD (paraphenylenediamine); LC-MS/MS (most sensitive)
supporting_tests: Serum CK (markedly elevated — rhabdomyolysis; >10,000 IU/L = severe); Creatinine (renal failure); Troponin (myocarditis); Histopathology: renal biopsy — ATN + myoglobin casts (pathognomonic)
toxic_threshold: PPD in blood/urine by HPLC (confirmation); Serum CK >10,000 IU/L = severe rhabdomyolysis; Creatinine rise = AKI; Myoglobin in urine (myoglobinuria) = naked-eye brown urine
critical_note: BROWN URINE (myoglobinuria) is a NAKED-EYE diagnostic clue — DSS should ask for urine colour as input (P10 optional parameter) and flag PPD as HIGH CONFIDENCE if brown urine is reported; Mortality 12–42% — flag as HIGH URGENCY in DSS output
priority_sample: Urine (brown — visual diagnostic + HPLC); Blood (CK; creatinine; troponin; PPD level); Gastric contents (early); Viscera (renal histopath PM)

screening_test: Sodium Dithionite Test on urine — BLUE colour = POSITIVE (highly specific; bedside test); Dithionite + NaOH + urine → blue if paraquat
confirmatory_test: HPLC or LC-MS/MS on urine / plasma for paraquat quantification; RIA (Radioimmunoassay) alternative
supporting_tests: Proudfoot nomogram — plasma paraquat level at specific time post- ingestion predicts survival probability; Lung function tests; Chest X-ray (pulmonary fibrosis — delayed); Renal function (serial)
toxic_threshold: Urine paraquat >1 mg/L = significant; Plasma level plotted on Proudfoot nomogram (time post-ingestion) determines prognosis; Pulmonary fibrosis on CXR = Phase 3 (very poor prognosis)
critical_note: Proudfoot nomogram is a KEY prognostic tool — DSS output for paraquat should include note: 'Plasma paraquat + time post-ingestion = Plot on Proudfoot nomogram for survival probability'; O2 supplementation WORSENS lung injury — include critical clinical warning in output
priority_sample: Urine (dithionite test — bedside FIRST); Blood plasma (Proudfoot nomogram); Lung tissue (PM — fibrosis histopath)

screening_test: Ferric Chloride Test (FeCl3) — violet-purple colour; Urine darkens to brown/black (phenol metabolites — visual clue; naked-eye diagnostic)
confirmatory_test: GC-MS on urine for phenol and conjugated metabolites (phenylglucuronide; phenylsulphate; parahydroquinone)
supporting_tests: Methaemoglobin level (carbolic acid oxidises Hb — metHb rises); Renal function (tubular damage); Liver enzymes; ABG (respiratory depression)
toxic_threshold: Urine phenol >250 mg/L = significant toxic exposure; Methaemoglobin >20% = significant; Phenylglucuronide and sulphate conjugates in urine = confirmation of exposure
critical_note: BIPHASIC CNS pattern (stimulation FIRST then depression) + CARBOLIC ODOUR + DARK URINE is near-pathognomonic — DSS should highlight this triad in output; MetHb measurement important — include methylene blue as treatment note if MetHb elevated
priority_sample: Gastric contents (URGENT — early); Urine (dark brown — visual + GC-MS); Blood (MetHb level); Viscera (liver, kidney)

screening_test: Dille–Koppanyi Test — purple to blue-violet (class-specific; cobalt acetate + isopropylamine)
confirmatory_test: GC-MS or HPLC on blood / urine for specific barbiturate identification (which barbiturate); Immunoassay screen (serum)
supporting_tests: Blood barbiturate level (quantitative — serial monitoring); EEG (isoelectric in severe cases); Temperature (hypothermia — hallmark); Skin biopsy (barb blisters — histopath)
toxic_threshold: Phenobarbitone blood: >40 µg/mL = toxic; >80 µg/mL = severe; >100 µg/mL = fatal; Amylobarbitone: >20 µg/mL = toxic; Hypothermia <35°C = severe barbiturate
critical_note: BULLOUS SKIN LESIONS (barb blisters) are pathognomonic for barbiturate poisoning — DSS should include this as a distinguishing clinical sign in the output explanation; Dille-Koppanyi does NOT identify WHICH barbiturate — GC-MS needed for that
priority_sample: Blood (serum — barbiturate level; URGENT); Urine (immunoassay screen); Gastric contents; Liver (post-mortem)

screening_test: Fujiwara Test — pink-red colour (chlorinated hydrocarbons with NaOH + pyridine)
confirmatory_test: GC-ECD (Gas Chromatography– Electron Capture Detector) — highly specific and sensitive for chlorinated compounds; GC-MS for compound identification and quantification
supporting_tests: Blood OCP levels (fat-soluble — serum); Adipose tissue analysis (chronic bioaccumulation); Liver enzymes; ECG (arrhythmia — catecholamine sensitisation)
toxic_threshold: No specific single threshold — OCP levels vary by compound; Tissue residue analysis (adipose) is most reliable for chronic; Adrenaline CONTRAINDICATED (risk of fatal ventricular arrhythmia in OCP poisoning)
critical_note: ADRENALINE IS ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in OCP poisoning (catecholamine sensitisation causes fatal VF) — DSS MUST include this clinical warning in output when OCP is predicted; Fat tissue is best sample for chronic exposure cases
priority_sample: Blood (serum — fat soluble; urgent); Adipose tissue biopsy (GOLD for chronic); Liver; Brain tissue; Breast milk (chronic)

screening_test: Silver nitrate paper test — BLACK (same as AlP; differentiate by: 1. Zinc assay 2. Scene evidence (Ratol grey/black pellets packaging)
confirmatory_test: GC headspace (phosphine) + ICP-MS or AAS for zinc in blood / tissue; Both tests together distinguish ZnP from AlP
supporting_tests: Blood phosphine; Blood/tissue zinc by AAS or ICP-MS; Metabolic acidosis (ABG); Liver function tests (zinc hepatotoxicity); Renal function
toxic_threshold: Blood phosphine detectable; Tissue zinc elevated (confirms zinc source vs aluminium); Metabolic acidosis pH <7.1 = severe; Liver enzymes elevated (zinc hepatotoxicity distinct from AlP)
critical_note: ZnP and AlP are CLINICALLY IDENTICAL — both give garlic odour and phosphine; KEY DSS DIFFERENTIATOR: if scene shows grey/black pellets (Ratol) = ZnP; white/grey tablets (Celphos) = AlP; ICP-MS zinc level distinguishes definitively; DSS output should show both as top candidates when garlic odour + ingestion + acute onset entered
priority_sample: Gastric contents (CRITICAL — phosphine); Blood (phosphine; zinc by ICP-MS); Liver; Lung (post-mortem)

screening_test: Clinical anticholinergic toxidrome identification; Physostigmine challenge (IV 1–2 mg) — reversal of delirium/mydriasis = confirms anticholinergic; NO colour reagent test for datura alkaloids
confirmatory_test: LC-MS/MS for atropine, scopolamine, hyoscyamine in blood / urine / gastric contents; GC-MS with derivatisation alternative
supporting_tests: Post-mortem vitreous humour (alkaloids stable PM — critical for homicidal robbery cases); Pupil diameter measurement (mydriasis >6mm = anticholinergic); Heart rate (tachycardia >120 = anticholinergic)
toxic_threshold: Blood atropine level varies — clinical correlation essential; Scopolamine causes AMNESIA — victim cannot recall events (forensic significance in robbery/DFSA cases); Vitreous humour: alkaloids detectable up to 72 hrs post-mortem
critical_note: FORENSIC SIGNIFICANCE IS HIGH — Datura is used in India for criminal robbing (victims given tea/food with Datura); DSS should flag: 'Scopolamine causes anterograde amnesia — consider DFSA/robbery context'; Vitreous humour is PRIMARY post-mortem sample for alkaloids
priority_sample: Blood (LC-MS/MS; urgent); Urine; Gastric contents; Vitreous humour (post-mortem — MOST IMPORTANT)

screening_test: Digoxin immunoassay (ELISA/CMIA) — oleandrin CROSS-REACTS with digoxin antibody — gives positive result (rapid screen); ECG (AV block type — hallmark cardiac glycoside)
confirmatory_test: LC-MS/MS for oleandrin, neriine (Nerium) or thevetin A&B (Thevetia) specifically in blood / bile / vitreous humour; Distinguishes from true digoxin poisoning
supporting_tests: ECG (AV block degree + arrhythmia type); Serum K+ (hyperkalemia — severity marker); Serum digoxin-like immunoreactive substance (DLIS) level; Liver enzymes (ischaemia)
toxic_threshold: Oleandrin by LC-MS/MS (confirmation); Serum K+ >6 mEq/L = severe toxicity; Complete AV block on ECG = severe; Digoxin immunoassay POSITIVE = rapid screen (cross-reaction)
critical_note: DIGOXIN IMMUNOASSAY POSITIVE in oleander/ calotropis cases due to cross-reaction — DSS should note this as a rapid screen option; BILE is an excellent post-mortem sample (higher concentration than blood); ECG AV block pattern is diagnostic clinically
priority_sample: Blood (oleandrin; LC-MS/MS; digoxin cross-reaction screen); Bile (GOLD for PM — higher concentration); Vitreous humour (PM); Gastric contents

screening_test: 20-min Whole Blood Clotting Test (WBCT20) — NORMAL clotting = Elapid (Cobra/Krait) not Viper; Neostigmine test (clinical — partial reversal = postsynaptic neurotoxin = Cobra)
confirmatory_test: Venom-specific ELISA (species identification from wound tissue or blood); Immunochromatographic strip test (rapid venom detection India)
supporting_tests: Arterial blood gas (respiratory failure — pO2 drop); Pulse oximetry (SpO2); Nerve conduction / EMG (neuromuscular block); ECG (cobra cardiotoxins)
toxic_threshold: WBCT20 NORMAL = Elapid (not haemotoxic); Venom ELISA species- specific (Cobra vs Krait vs Viper); SpO2 <90% = respiratory failure — urgent ventilator need; Neostigmine reversal = postsynaptic block
critical_note: WBCT20 is the PRIMARY bedside test distinguishing Elapid (neurotoxic) from Viper (haemotoxic) — DSS should recommend WBCT20 as FIRST test when snakebite is predicted; KRAIT bites are often nocturnal with minimal local reaction — DSS should flag this pattern; Wound tissue ELISA is best for PM species ID
priority_sample: Blood (WBCT20; coagulation; ASV response); Wound tissue (venom ELISA — species ID); Arterial blood gas; Vitreous humour (PM)

screening_test: WBCT20 — blood does NOT CLOT at 20 min = Viper envenomation (PATHOGNOMONIC bedside test — no lab needed); Spontaneous gum/wound bleeding = clinical
confirmatory_test: Venom-specific ELISA (Russell's viper vs Echis differentiation from wound tissue / blood); Immunochromatographic strip test (rapid)
supporting_tests: Full coagulation panel: PT, aPTT, fibrinogen, d-dimer, platelet count (DIC severity); Urine microscopy (RBC casts = tubular injury); Serum creatinine (AKI — serial); Post-mortem: adrenal haemorrhage (Russell's viper — classic gross PM)
toxic_threshold: WBCT20 non-clotting = diagnostic; Fibrinogen <100 mg/dL = severe DIC; Platelets <50,000/µL = severe; Creatinine rise = AKI (Russell's viper specific delayed AKI; dialysis required in up to 30% cases)
critical_note: WBCT20 is ZERO COST bedside test requiring no equipment — DSS should highlight this; RUSSELL'S VIPER causes delayed AKI (days after bite) — DSS should flag serial renal monitoring; Adrenal haemorrhage at post-mortem = classic Russell's viper finding — include in PM output
priority_sample: Blood (WBCT20 — URGENT; coagulation panel; CBC); Urine (haematuria; RBC casts); Wound tissue (ELISA); Kidney (histopath PM); Adrenal (gross PM)

screening_test: Clinical + petroleum odour on breath/vomit (STRONGLY PRESENT; highly reliable); Chest X-ray (bilateral infiltrates = aspiration pneumonitis — rapid radiological screen)
confirmatory_test: GC-HS (Gas Chromatography –Headspace) on gastric contents / blood for hydrocarbon identification and quantification; Specific hydrocarbon profile by GC-MS
supporting_tests: Arterial blood gas (PaO2 drop — hypoxia); CBC (leucocytosis from chemical pneumonitis); Chest X-ray (pattern and progression); Liver function tests (hepatotoxicity — halogenated HC)
toxic_threshold: PaO2 <60 mmHg = significant hypoxia; Chest X-ray bilateral infiltrates within 4–6 hrs = aspiration pneumonitis (aspiration occurs during ingestion OR vomiting); No specific blood level threshold
critical_note: GASTRIC LAVAGE IS CONTRAINDICATED — makes aspiration worse; DSS MUST include this critical clinical warning in output when kerosene is predicted; This is primarily a PAEDIATRIC poison in India — DSS should flag age group context when child age group is input
priority_sample: Gastric contents (GC-HS); Blood (ABG; SpO2 — urgent); Lung tissue (histopath — PM); Chest X-ray (imaging)

screening_test: VISUAL — blue-green vomit/gastric contents (near-pathognomonic; naked-eye diagnostic); Urine brown/red (haemoglobinuria — visual)
confirmatory_test: Serum copper by AAS or ICP-MS; Urine copper excretion (24-hr collection); Liver copper content (AAS — post-mortem)
supporting_tests: LDH, haptoglobin, free haemoglobin (haemolysis panel); Methaemoglobin level; LFTs (hepatic damage); Renal function (serial); Histopathology: renal tubular haemoglobin casts (pathognomonic)
toxic_threshold: Serum copper >200 µg/dL = toxic (normal <120 µg/dL); LDH markedly elevated (haemolysis); MetHb >20% = significant; Haemoglobinuria present = significant intravascular haemolysis
critical_note: BLUE-GREEN GASTRIC CONTENTS is the single most specific visual sign in Indian forensic toxicology — no other common poison produces this; DSS should output 'Blue-green vomit = HIGH CONFIDENCE Copper Sulphate'; Methylene blue for MetHb if >30% — include as treatment note in DSS output
priority_sample: Blood (serum copper; haemolysis panel; MetHb); Urine (haemoglobin; copper); Gastric contents (blue-green — visual + AAS); Liver (AAS — PM)

screening_test: NO specific colour or reagent test available for aconite; Clinical diagnosis: immediate oral/facial paraesthesia + ventricular arrhythmia on ECG; ECG (continuous) is the closest 'screen'
confirmatory_test: LC-MS/MS for aconitine, mesaconitine, hypaconitine in blood / gastric contents / urine; GC-MS with derivatisation also usable; Bile (post-mortem — high concentration)
supporting_tests: ECG (continuous — VF/VT detection; AV block); Electrolytes (K+; Mg2+); ABG; No specific biochemical marker — alkaloid toxicology is definitive
toxic_threshold: Aconitine in blood >1 ng/mL = toxic; Alkaloids stable post-mortem in tissues; Ventricular fibrillation on ECG = immediately life-threatening; Death can occur within 1–6 hrs
critical_note: NO GROSS POST-MORTEM FINDINGS — aconite leaves no visible pathology at autopsy; TOXICOLOGY IS THE ONLY WAY TO DIAGNOSE post-mortem — DSS must emphasise this; LC-MS/MS is mandatory (no colour test exists); PARAESTHESIA as the first symptom is unique to aconite among Indian poisons
priority_sample: Blood (LC-MS/MS — URGENT for alive patients); Gastric contents; Urine; Bile (PM — GOLD; high conc); Liver

screening_test: Digoxin immunoassay — calotropin CROSS- REACTS with digoxin antibody (rapid screen); ECG (cardiac glycoside arrhythmia — AV block; bradycardia)
confirmatory_test: LC-MS/MS for calotropin, calactin, uscharidin in blood / gastric contents; Distinguishes calotropis from oleander and digoxin specifically
supporting_tests: Serum K+ (hyperkalemia — severity marker); ECG (AV block degree; VT/VF); Serum digoxin-like level; Liver enzymes (ischaemia); Respiratory function (late stage)
toxic_threshold: Calotropin by LC-MS/MS (confirmation); Serum K+ >6 mEq/L = severe; Complete AV block = severe; Digoxin assay POSITIVE = cross-reaction rapid screen only (not specific for calotropis vs oleander vs true digoxin)
critical_note: CALOTROPIS and OLEANDER both cause cardiac glycoside toxicity and both cross-react with digoxin immunoassay — LC-MS/MS is needed to distinguish between them; DSS should output BOTH calotropis AND oleander as differential when cardiac glycoside pattern is present; Scene evidence (plant identification) is critical differentiator
priority_sample: Blood (calotropin; LC-MS/MS; digoxin cross-reaction screen); Gastric contents; Urine; Viscera (PM)

screening_test: Urine immunoassay (ELISA) — detects BZD class (screen); Flumazenil IV challenge (0.2 mg) — patient wakes within 1–2 min = BZD confirmed (diagnostic + therapeutic)
confirmatory_test: GC-MS or LC-MS/MS on blood / urine for specific BZD identification (which BZD: diazepam vs alprazolam vs clonazepam etc.); Hair analysis by LC-MS/MS (DFSA cases — detects back 3–6 months)
supporting_tests: Blood BZD level (quantitative); EtOH level (co-ingestion synergy); Liver function tests; Hair analysis (DFSA investigations — critical for drug-facilitated assault cases in India)
toxic_threshold: BZD urine immunoassay: positive up to 72 hrs (short-acting) or 7 days (long-acting like diazepam); Hair analysis detects exposure back to 3–6 months; Flumazenil reversal = rapid BZD confirmation
critical_note: DFSA (Drug-Facilitated Sexual Assault) context — DSS should flag BZD + scopolamine (Datura) as DFSA substances when amnesia is a reported symptom; Hair analysis is CRITICAL for DFSA cases — add to DSS output when DFSA context is suspected; Flumazenil is both diagnostic and therapeutic
priority_sample: Blood (BZD level; immunassay); Urine (up to 7 days); Hair (DFSA — 3–6 months detection; CRITICAL for DFSA); Gastric lavage

screening_test: Ferric Chloride Test — violet-purple colour (phenolic compound; non-specific screen); Serum paracetamol level at 4 HRS post-ingestion — plotted on Rumack- Matthew nomogram (CRITICAL decision tool)
confirmatory_test: Serum paracetamol level by immunoassay or HPLC (quantitative; time-critical — draw at 4 hrs post-ingestion for nomogram); Liver histopathology: centrilobular (zone 3) necrosis = pathognomonic post-mortem finding
supporting_tests: AST, ALT (liver damage panel — serial); INR (coagulopathy marker); Bilirubin; Serum creatinine (hepatorenal syndrome); Blood glucose (hypoglycaemia); Arterial blood gas (metabolic acidosis)
toxic_threshold: Serum paracetamol: >150 µg/mL at 4 hrs = NAC treatment required (per Rumack nomogram); AST/ALT >1000 IU/L = significant hepatic injury; INR >2 at 24 hrs = severe; Histopath: zone 3 centrilobular necrosis = pathognomonic
critical_note: RUMACK-MATTHEW NOMOGRAM is the CENTRAL DECISION TOOL — serum level at EXACTLY 4 hrs post- ingestion determines treatment; DSS MUST include: 'Draw serum paracetamol at 4 hours — plot on Rumack-Matthew nomogram for NAC decision'; PATIENT APPEARS WELL in first 24 hrs — DSS should flag: 'Minimal early symptoms do NOT indicate safety — nomogram level is the only reliable guide'
priority_sample: Blood (serum paracetamol level at 4 hrs — CRITICAL TIMING; LFTs; INR; creatinine); Liver (zone 3 histopath — post-mortem; pathognomonic); Gastric contents
